courses.course_outline: The course introduces students to the subject of Astronomy and our place in the universe from the small scales of the Earth-Sun-Moon system to the large scales of distant galaxies. It aims to provide insight into how we study astrophysical objects via EM radiation and telescopes (theory) as well as providing a high-level overview of objects in the universe, moving outwards from our solar system, to stars and stellar remnants, our galaxy and others, dark matter and cosmology, and the study of the universe at the largest scales. The course is open to all interested students as well as providing a solid introduction to those wishing to continue in astrophysics.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 5th period. Practicals: One compulsory practical or tutorial per week, Wednesday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.notes: Two practical sessions held in the Planetarium of Iziko Museums of Cape Town, plus one evening trip to the South African Astronomical Observatory, Cape Town, by arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance at lectures and compulsory attendance at Wednesday afternoon sessions and submission of bi-weekly problem sets and poster project; class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record: 50%; final examination 2 hours: 50%. Sub-minimum: 40% for final examination.

courses.prerequisites: PHY1004W, MAM1000W or both MAM1031F & MAM1032S (or equivalent), CSC1015F/S
courses.course_outline: This course presents an introduction to the theoretical aspects of modern astrophysics. The key objective is to illustrate the application of physical laws in an astronomical context and to explain how we know what we do about the universe and its constituents. Subject matter includes: Celestial mechanics; radiation laws; blackbody radiation, Planck function and approximations; magnitudes; the hydrogen atom; stellar spectroscopy; stellar evolution and remnants; special relativity; the Earth-Moon system; the Solar system; extrasolar planets; stellar motions; the Milky Way and other galaxies; the extragalactic distance scale; large scale structure; Newtonian cosmology.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period, Term 2 and 4. Tutorials: 10 Compulsory tutorial/practical sessions over the year, Wednesday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.notes: One field trip to the South African Astronomical Observatory, Sutherland.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials; satisfactory completion levels of quizzes, tests and assignments and class mark of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Three class tests count 25%; 4 compulsory tutorials/practicals count 20%; regular quizzes count 5%; one 2-hour final examination in November counts 50%. Sub-minimum requirement of 40% for final examination.

prerequisites.pre_requisites: PHY1004W and MAM1000W or both MAM1031F & MAM1032S (or equivalent); or PHY1023H and MAM1005H; CSC1015F/S

courses.course_outline: This course combines a large practical component (radio and optical astronomy practicals) with theoretical background in astronomical techniques, instrumentation and data analysis. Topics include: Positional astronomy: time systems, spherical astronomy, co-ordinate systems and conversions, astrometry; Detection systems: interaction of radiation and matter, ultraviolet and optical detectors; Optics and telescope design; Multi-wavelength astronomy: infrared, ultraviolet, x-ray and gamma-ray astronomy, fundamentals of radio astronomy; Observational techniques: photometry and spectroscopy; Orthodox statistics: probability distributions, Chi-squared distribution, propagation of errors; Stochastic processes and noise: photon noise.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period, Term 1 and 3. Tutorials: Five over the year, Wednesday, 14h00–17h00, by arrangement. Practicals: Evening observing practicals using the UCT teaching telescopes, by arrangement.
courses.notes: One observational radio astronomy project and one observational optical astronomy project, by arrangement. One field trip to South African Astronomical Observatory, Sutherland.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials. Attendance at all fieldwork practicals. Class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Two class tests 15%; 6 tutorials over the year (astronomical data analysis and statistical techniques) count 20%; observing projects, practicals and quizzes together count 40%; one 2-hour theoretical examination counts 25%.

courses.prerequisites: AST2002H and AST2003H, PHY2004W, MAM2010F and MAM2011F
courses.course_outline: This course introduces fundamental concepts such as radiative transfer and opacity to explain the observed spectroscopic and photometric signatures of stars. Students will interpret the observed intrinsic properties of stars through a theoretical understanding of the energy production inside stars and the propagation of electromagnetic radiation from the stellar core through its interior to the stellar surface. The life cycle of stars is considered in great detail, from the collapse of an interstellar gas cloud to the end products of stellar evolution: white dwarfs, neutron stars and black holes. Includes an observational component using the modern teaching observatory on campus.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period. Practicals: One practical or tutorial per week, Wednesday, 14h00–17h00.
courses.notes: Two evening observing sessions at the UCT teaching observatory, by prior arrangement.
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory attendance at lectures and tutorials; class record of at least 35%.
courses.assessment: Class record 50% (includes two class tests, tutorials, weekly quizzes and practicals); one 2-hour final examination 50%. Sub-minimum requirement of 40% for final examination.

courses.prerequisites: AST2002H and AST2003H, PHY2004W, MAM2010F and MAM2011F
courses.course_outline: The aim of this course is to provide a broad introduction to galactic & extragalactic astrophysics and cosmology. The first part of the course (term 3) focuses on Galactic astronomy, and the second part (term 4) focuses on extragalactic astronomy. Topics include the composition and kinematics of the Milky Way, observations and characterisation of galaxies, active galaxies, galaxy interactions and environments and galaxy evolution. Current research and hot topics are also discussed. A further aim is to develop observing, data analysis skills, scientific presentation and writing skills.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Friday, 2nd period. Tutorials/Practicals: One practical or tutorial per week, Wednesday, 14h00–17h00; one observing practical in Term 4.
courses.dp_requirements: Class record of at least 35%. Calculated from all assessments completed within 4 weeks of deadline and lecture/tutorial attendance up until the date by which DP must be achieved. Attendance at practical observing session.
courses.assessment: Class record 50% (includes two class tests, assignment sheets, online quizzes, observing practical, Hot Topics assignment, attendance at lectures and tutorials); one 2-hour final examination 50%. Sub-minimum requirement of 40% for final examination.

courses.prerequisites: AST3002F and AST3003S or PHY3004W or MAM3040W or equivalent. Candidates with an Engineering background will also be considered. Enrolments limited to 20 students. Candidates must satisfy the Steering Committee of sufficient background in Mathematics and Physics. Application must be made before 31 August of the preceding year.
courses.course_outline: The Honours course in Astrophysics & Space Science consists of courses presented by distinguished South African researchers from research institutions participating in NASSP. Includes a theory component (spectroscopy, electrodynamics, general relativity, general astrophysics, galaxies, computational physics, astrophysical fluid dynamics and computational methods) and an observational techniques component (optical and infrared astronomy and radio astronomy). Students also complete a mini research project and a main research project, and attend fieldtrips to national facilities.
courses.notes: Course code AST4007W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for AST4008W (coursework component, 128 NQF credits) and AST4009W (research project, 32 NQF credits).
courses.dp_requirements: Satisfactory lecture attendance (minimum 50%); class record of at least 40%.
courses.assessment: Examinations count 40%; class record 40%; research project 20% of final result. Project component must be passed at 50%. Coursework and project codes must each be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty.

This course consists of an investigation of an approved research topic on which a minor dissertation must be presented for formal assessment. The minor dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature.
courses.notes: Part of the National Astrophysics & Space Science Programme (NASSP).
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. Coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.semester: First Semester / Full Year (F suffix; dissertation in July)
courses.prerequisites: Honours graduates in Astronomy and Space Science (AST4007W), Physics (PHY4000W, PHY4001W, PHY4002W) or equivalent, and Engineering. Minimum pass mark of 60% in the Honours degree. Admission subject to approval of the Steering Committee; application must be made before 30 June of the preceding year.
courses.course_outline: A selection of advanced topics presented by distinguished South African researchers from research institutions participating in NASSP. Topics vary from year to year but usually include cataclysmic variables, extragalactic astronomy, space technology, hot topics in cosmology, advanced general relativity, high energy astrophysics, observational cosmology, geomagnetism and aeronomy, plasma physics and magnetohydrodynamics.
courses.notes: Part of the National Astrophysics & Space Science Programme (NASSP). Students enrol and pay fees for the coursework component (AST5003F) at the start of their first year. Those remaining at UCT to complete the minor dissertation (AST5001W, MAM5005W or PHY5003W) enrol for the minor dissertation component in July.
courses.assessment: On average, examinations of individual modules count 60% of the final result; marked practical work counts 40%.

courses.prerequisites: Core modules of the Master's in Data Science course.
courses.course_outline: This course introduces students to various aspects of data intensive astrophysics, ranging from data visualisation and complex databases to advanced statistical tools for astronomical data analysis and computational astrophysics. At the core of this module are examples in modern data-intensive astrophysics derived from global data challenges around MeerKAT, the Square Kilometre Array (SKA), associated projects in radio astronomy, and other large multi-wavelength surveys. Students will be introduced to Bayesian statistics in astronomy, visualising large data cubes, optimising database operations in the presence of multi-dimensional data, data mining and discovery tools, and the role of large-scale simulations to interpret the significance of astronomical observations.
courses.assessment: Two projects: 25% each; practical 'take-home' data science examination: 50%. A sub-minimum of 50% for each of the projects and examination component is required.

courses.prerequisites: Successful completion of the coursework component of the Masters course in Data Science.
courses.course_outline: The research component of the degree is based on a 90 credit dissertation. The topic of the research will be based on an analysis of large data sets from Astronomy.
courses.assessment: The minor dissertation must be presented for formal examination. Coursework and minor dissertation each count 50% towards the degree; each must be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.course_outline: The PhD is a research degree on an advanced topic under supervision. The thesis must constitute a substantial contribution to knowledge in the chosen subject, must show evidence of original investigation and give a full statement of the literature on the subject. The PhD degree demands that the candidate is able to conduct independent research on his/her own initiative. The candidate must demonstrate they are at the academic forefront in the topic selected, that the work is original and advances knowledge in the relevant field.
